perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the United States of America, State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of England.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to England creates a conflict, as the agreement references 'United States securities laws' in section 7, which would not be directly applicable or enforceable under English law.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change makes the applicability of US securities laws uncertain, conflicting with the intention to comply with them stated later in the agreement (section 7), and creating ambiguity about which jurisdiction's laws apply. The agreement is between an US and UK company, so under US conflict of laws, there must be some nexus to the chosen jurisdiction to enforce it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the agreement is governed by the laws of the United States of America, State of California. The changed text states that the agreement is governed by the laws of England. The Perturbation Explanation states that the agreement references 'United States securities laws' in section 7, which would not be directly applicable or enforceable under English law. The Law Explanation mentions US conflict of laws principles, requiring a nexus to the chosen jurisdiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute. Here, the conflicting legal rules come from the U.S. and England. Because the contract explicitly references US securities laws in section 7, but chooses the law of England in the governing law clause, this introduces a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, which occurs when a contract uses terms that do not match their statutory meaning, leading to non-compliance. This makes the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Except as provided herein, the Parties agree that any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance or breach of this Agreement shall be settled by arbitration to be held in San Diego County, California, USA in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules then in effect for the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as provided herein, the Parties agree that any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance or breach of this Agreement shall be settled by private negotiation only.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the dispute resolution mechanism from arbitration to private negotiation removes the legal recourse of arbitration, which is a legally recognized form of dispute resolution under both state and federal law. This contradicts the expectations and legal rights associated with standard contract enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: By replacing arbitration with only private negotiation, the modified text undermines the enforceability of dispute resolution as contemplated under the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA), hindering the parties' ability to legally compel a binding resolution to conflicts. The FAA governs the enforcement of arbitration agreements in contracts involving interstate commerce, and removing the arbitration clause limits the avenues of dispute resolution.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces mandatory arbitration with private negotiation, directly contradicting the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA). Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 U.S. Code § 2, states that written arbitration agreements in contracts involving commerce are 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable.' By eliminating arbitration, the changed text undermines this legal provision, restricting dispute resolution options and contradicting the FAA's intent to ensure the enforceability of arbitration agreements. The original text's commitment to arbitration is a legally recognized mechanism, while the modified text removes this right, leading to a clear legal contradiction and non-compliance with the FAA.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information, technical data or knowledge which: a. is already known to the Receiving Party; b. is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of the Receiving Party; c. is rightfully received from a third party without restrictions and without breach of this Agreement; d. is independently developed by the Receiving Party; e. is approved for release by written authorization of the disclosing party; or f. if orally disclosed by one party to the other and within thirty (30) days after the oral disclosure, the disclosing party does not so identify it in writing as Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include information, technical data or knowledge which: a. is already known to the Receiving Party; b. becomes publicly known, regardless of the Receiving Party's actions; c. is received from any third party; d. is independently developed by the Receiving Party; e. is approved for release without written authorization; or f. if disclosed orally by one party to the other, and is never identified in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By broadening the exceptions to what constitutes confidential information, specifically removing the 'no wrongful act' clause, allowing information received from *any* third party to be excluded, allowing release without written authorization, and waiving any written identification requirement after oral disclosure, this modification severely weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws. It makes the agreement unenforceable, as there is no reasonable protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text significantly reduces the scope of what's considered 'Confidential Information,' potentially undermining trade secret protection afforded by the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). By removing safeguards against wrongful disclosure and broadening exceptions, the contract could fail to protect legitimate trade secrets, conflicting with the intent and purpose of the DTSA to prevent misappropriation of valuable business information. The DTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and this modification does not provide these.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the protection of confidential information. The removal of 'no wrongful act' and the allowance of information received from 'any' third party as exceptions, along with waiving written authorization and identification requirements, undermines trade secret protection. The LLM's law explanation states that 'The DTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and this modification does not provide these.' This aligns with the perturbation explanation stating that the modification makes the agreement unenforceable because there is no reasonable protection of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is non-existent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, suggesting the importance of protecting business information, which the changed text fails to do.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that from the date of first receipt, and for a period of three (3) years following the last disclosure of Confidential Information, the party receiving the Confidential Information (the “Receiving Party”) shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any other person, or entity, or use for its own benefit except as provided in this Agreement and shall use the same degree of care to avoid publication or dissemination of such information as it does for its own confidential information which it does not desire to have published or disseminated.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that from the date of first receipt, the Receiving Party shall not be responsible for maintaining confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: Removing any obligation for the receiving party to maintain confidentiality completely negates the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, rendering the entire agreement meaningless. This directly contradicts the fundamental principle of protecting confidential information, which is the core of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification completely eliminates the confidentiality obligation, which is at odds with the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) that protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets. By explicitly stating that the Receiving Party has no responsibility for maintaining confidentiality, the agreement offers no protection for trade secrets, conflicting with CUTSA's provisions for safeguarding confidential business information. The CUTSA defines what constitutes misappropriation, and removing the confidentiality obligation facilitates such misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and the principles outlined in the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text establishes an obligation to maintain confidentiality, while the changed text explicitly states that the Receiving Party is not responsible for maintaining confidentiality. This removes the core protection for confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code, Division 4, Title 5, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While this snippet itself doesn't contain the specific legal text being violated, it establishes that CUTSA is relevant. Given the explicit removal of the confidentiality obligation, and the purpose of CUTSA is to protect trade secrets, a legal contradiction exists. Snippet 2 is missing but Snippet 1 does establish the relevance of the CUTSA.
